name: Problems on Ages — Past, Present and Future
whatIsIt: Age problems are word puzzles you turn into simple equations. The golden rule: everyone ages by the SAME amount, so the DIFFERENCE between two people's ages never changes. To go back t years, subtract t from each present age; to go forward t years, add t. Let the unknown present age be x, translate the sentence, and solve.
whyNeeded: These 'father is thrice the son' puzzles are a staple of SSC, Bank, Railway and campus aptitude papers. They also train the core skill of turning ordinary English into an equation — the heart of all word-problem solving.
realLifeUses: Working out a parent's age from clues about a child's age
Family riddles: 'ten years ago I was twice your age'
Checking eligibility dates (minimum age from a year gap)
SSC / Bank / Railway quantitative aptitude questions

vocabulary.meaning: The age right now — usually the unknown x you solve for.
How many years apart two people are — this stays the SAME forever.
Subtract t from each present age.
Add t to each present age (hence = in the future).

story.title: Guess Appa's Age
story.text: Nila is 10. 'Appa, you're four times my age!' So Appa is 40 now. 'But in 5 years, will you still be four times me?' Appa smiles: 'Add 5 to both — I'll be 45, you'll be 15. 45 is only three times 15, not four. Our AGE GAP stays 30 forever, but the ratio changes as we both grow.' Nila realises the difference is the trick — always 30 years between them.
story.extractedProblem: Nila is 10 and Appa is four times as old. How old will Appa be in 5 years, and is he still four times Nila?
story.answerInStory: Appa is 40 now, 45 in 5 years; Nila will be 15, so 45 is 3× — the ratio changed, but the gap stayed 30.

bars.label: The gap never changes; the ratio does

visual.caption: Both people age the same amount, so the age DIFFERENCE is constant. Only the ratio between them changes over time.

teachingGallery.title: Let x = present age, translate, solve
teachingGallery.note: Same time passes for everyone → the difference is fixed. 'ago' subtracts, 'hence' adds.

examples.caption: If son is 10, father 30, gap 20 — the gap is 2x.
Going back 5 years keeps the gap the same.

standardMethod.summary: Name the present age x, shift by t for past/future, write the sentence as an equation, and solve.
steps: Let the unknown present age be x (write the other ages in terms of x).
For 't years ago', use (x − t); for 't years hence', use (x + t).
Translate the clue into an equation and solve for x.
Use the fixed age difference as a quick check.

alternateMethods.name: Fixed-difference shortcut
alternateMethods.whenToUse: When two ratios (now and later) are given.
alternateMethods.example: Ages 3:4 now, 5:6 later — the constant gap pins down the actual ages.

workedExamples.problem: A father is 4 times his son's age. The son is 8. How old is the father?
The sum of a mother's and daughter's ages is 50, and the mother is 4 times the daughter. Find the daughter's age.

trickPractice.trick: Same time passes → age gap is constant
trickPractice.intro: Set x = present age, add/subtract t for future/past, solve the little equation.

questions.q: Son 12, father is 3× as old. Father's age?
A is 25 now. How old in 7 years?
B is 30 now. How old 8 years ago?
Sum of two ages is 60, ratio 2 : 1. Older age?

commonMistakes.mistake: Adding the t years to only one person when moving to the past or future.
commonMistakes.fix: Time passes for EVERYONE. If you go 5 years ahead, add 5 to every age in the problem — not just one.

commonMistakes.mistake: Assuming the ratio between two ages stays the same over the years.
commonMistakes.fix: The DIFFERENCE stays constant, not the ratio. A father twice his son's age won't stay twice as old — but the gap between them never changes.

easy.q: Daughter is 9; mother is 5 times as old. Mother's age?
If 6 years pass, whose age changes?

medium.q: Sum of ages of A and B is 44, and A is 3 times B. Find B.
Ravi is 40. Five years ago he was how old?

challenge.q: A father is 30 years older than his son. In 10 years, how much older will he be?
A man is twice his son's age now. Twenty years later he will be…

questions.q: Son is 7, father is 6 times as old. Father's age?
Sum of two ages is 36, ratio 2 : 1. Older age?
Over the years, what stays constant between two people?
Meera is 28. How old will she be in 12 years?
A is 45, B is 15. What is their age difference?

teachBackPrompt: Teach a friend the golden rule of age problems: the DIFFERENCE stays constant but the ratio changes. Use a parent aged 40 and child aged 10, jump 5 years ahead for both, and show why the '4 times' becomes '3 times'.

revisionCard.summary: Let present age = x; write others in terms of x. 'Ago' subtracts t, 'hence' adds t — to EVERYONE. The age difference is constant; the ratio is not. Translate the sentence to an equation and solve. Quick check: recompute the fixed gap.

gameMission.title: Age Detective
gameMission.brief: Crack every family riddle! Name the unknown, shift the years for everyone, and solve — while dodging the 'ratio stays the same' trap.

realLifeProject: Ask two family members their ages and write three true clues (a sum, a difference, and a 'years ago' fact). Give the clues to someone else and let them solve for the ages — just like an exam age problem.

rememberIt.hook: The age GAP never changes; the ratio does
rememberIt.unpack: Let x = now. 'Ago' subtracts t, 'hence' adds t — to everyone. Difference is constant.

formulas.name: Constant gap
formulas.whenToUse: Two ratios given at different times.

studentQuestions.q: Why doesn't the ratio stay the same?
studentQuestions.a: Adding the same number of years to both ages changes the ratio (40:10 is 4×, but 45:15 is only 3×) even though the 30-year gap is unchanged.

studentQuestions.q: How do I start a hard age problem?
studentQuestions.a: Name the present age x, write everyone else in terms of x, then turn each sentence into an equation and solve.
